coredata.dc:title: Fabrication and application of molecularly imprinted polymer doped carbon dots coated silica stationary phase
coredata.dc:creator: Chai, Peijun
Geng, Xuhui
Zhu, Ruirui
Wu, Wenpu
Wang, Xuesong
Li, Jinhua
Fu, Longwen
Wang, Hongdan
Liu, Wanhui
Chen, Lingxin
Song, Zhihua
coredata.dc:description: Facing the difficulties in chromatographic separation of polar compounds, this investigation devotes to developing novel stationary phase. Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) have aroused wide attention, owing to their outstanding selectivity, high stability, and low cost. In this work, a novel stationary phase based on carbon dots (CDs), MIP layer, and silica beads was synthesized to exploit high selectivity of MIPs, excellent physicochemical property of CDs, and outstanding chromatographic performances of silica microspheres simultaneously. The MIP doped CDs coated silica (MIP-CDs/SiO2) stationary phase was systematically characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area measurement, and carbon elemental analysis. Furthermore, the chromatographic performance of the MIP-CDs/SiO2 column was thoroughly assessed by using a wide variety of compounds (including nucleosides, sulfonamides, benzoic acids, and some other antibiotics). Meanwhile, the separation efficiency of the MIP-CDs/SiO2 stationary phase was superior to other kinds of stationary phases (e.g. nonimprinted NIP-CDs/SiO2, MIP/SiO2, and C18–SiO2). The results demonstrated that MIP-CDs/SiO2 column exhibited best performance in terms of chromatographic separation. For all tested compounds, the resolution value was not less than 1.60, and the column efficiency of MIP-CDs/SiO2 for thymidine was 22,740 plates/m. The results further indicate that the MIP-CDs/SiO2 column can combine the good properties of MIP, CDs, with those of silica microbeads. Therefore, the developed MIP-CDs/SiO2 stationary phase can be applied in the separation science and chromatography-based techniques.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Molecularly imprinted polymer
Silica microbeads
Liquid chromatographic stationary phases
Separation performances evaluation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of nucleosides in plasma and urine
Chromatographic evaluation of the MIP-CDs/SiO2 stationary phase
Separation efficiency of different columns for nucleosides
Chromatographic performance of MIP-CDs/SiO2 column for antibiotics, and benzoic acids
Stability and reproducibility of the MIP-CDs/SiO2 column
Analysis of nucleosides in plasma and urine

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diagrammatic sketch of the synthesis of MIP-CDs/SiO2 stationary phase.
SEM images of the (A) MIP-CDs/SiO2 and (B) NIP-CDs/SiO2; TEM images of the (C) propenyl-modified silica, (D) MIP-CDs/SiO2, (E) NIP-CDs/SiO2, (F) MIP/SiO2.
(A) Particle size distribution of the materials; (B) Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of the materials in this work.

Plots of relative retention factor (k’) versus composition of mobile phases for MIP-CDs/SiO2 column. Chromatographic condition: (A) MeOH/aqueous solution containing 0.2% HAC (v/v), (B) MeOH/aqueous solution with different concentrations of HAC (0.3/99.7, v/v); flow rate: 0.2 mL/min; injection volume: 1 μL; UV detection wavelength, 260 nm.

Chromatograms of nucleosides on various columns: (A) MIP-CDs/SiO2, (B) MIP/SiO2, (C) NIP-CDs/SiO2, (D) C18–SiO2, (E) Diol-SiO2, and (F) Amino-SiO2. Analytes: 1. cytidine, 2. adenine, 3. thymidine, 4. stavudine, 5. zidovudine. Chromatographic condition: MeOH/aqueous solution containing 0.3% HAC (0.3/99.7, v/v); flow rate, 0.2 mL/min; column temperature, 30 °C; injection volume, 1 μL; UV detection wavelength, 260 nm.

Comparisons of different columns for separation of sulfonamides. Analytes: 1. sulfanilamide, 2. sulfacetamide, 3. sulfapyridine, 4. sulfamethoxypyridazine, 5. sulfamethoxazole. Chromatographic condition: ACN/aqueous solution with 0.3% HAC (5/95, v/v); flow rate, 0.2 mL/min; column temperature, 30 °C; injection volume, 1 μL; UV detection wavelength, 270 nm.

Comparisons of the chromatograms of analytes (1. amoxicillin crystalline, 2. cephalexin hydrate, 3. oxytetracycline hydrochloride, 4. norfloxacin) on several columns. Chromatographic condition: ACN/aqueous solution with 0.8% HAC (1/99, v/v); flow rate, 0.2 mL/min; column temperature, 30 °C; injection volume, 1 μL; UV detection wavelength, 270 nm.

Stability of MIP-CDs/SiO2 column for separation of nucleosides. Chromatographic condition: MeOH/aqueous solution with 0.3% HAC (0.3/99.7, v/v); flow rate, 0.2 mL/min; column temperature, 30 °C; injection volume, 1 μL; UV detection wavelength, 260 nm.
Comparison of recently reported nanomaterial-based chromatographic materials for the separation of analytes。.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Si-CQDs: carbon quantum dots (CQDs)-functionalized silica; C18-CDs decorated silica: octadecylamine-derived carbon dots; Sil-Glc-NCDs: glucose-based nitrogen-doped carbon dots (Glc-NCDs)-bonded silica stationary phase; Sil-ImCDs: Imidazolium ionic liquids-derived carbon dots-modified silica; Sil-MIm: imidazolium ionic liquid-functionalized stationary phase; Sil-NH2: aminopropyl silica; Sil-Glc-CDs: glucose-derived carbon dots-modified on silica; Tf: tailing factor; S: Symmetry factor; As: asymmetry factor.

head.ce:title: Fabrication and application of molecularly imprinted polymer doped carbon dots coated silica stationary phase

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Facing the difficulties in chromatographic separation of polar compounds, this investigation devotes to developing novel stationary phase. Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) have aroused wide attention, owing to their outstanding selectivity, high stability, and low cost. In this work, a novel stationary phase based on carbon dots (CDs), MIP layer, and silica beads was synthesized to exploit high selectivity of MIPs, excellent physicochemical property of CDs, and outstanding chromatographic performances of silica microspheres simultaneously. The MIP doped CDs coated silica (MIP-CDs/SiO2) stationary phase was systematically characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area measurement, and carbon elemental analysis. Furthermore, the chromatographic performance of the MIP-CDs/SiO2 column was thoroughly assessed by using a wide variety of compounds (including nucleosides, sulfonamides, benzoic acids, and some other antibiotics). Meanwhile, the separation efficiency of the MIP-CDs/SiO2 stationary phase was superior to other kinds of stationary phases (e.g. nonimprinted NIP-CDs/SiO2, MIP/SiO2, and C18–SiO2). The results demonstrated that MIP-CDs/SiO2 column exhibited best performance in terms of chromatographic separation. For all tested compounds, the resolution value was not less than 1.60, and the column efficiency of MIP-CDs/SiO2 for thymidine was 22,740 plates/m. The results further indicate that the MIP-CDs/SiO2 column can combine the good properties of MIP, CDs, with those of silica microbeads. Therefore, the developed MIP-CDs/SiO2 stationary phase can be applied in the separation science and chromatography-based techniques.

MIP-CDs/SiO2 stationary phase was synthesized by the combination of MIPs, CDs, and silica microspheres. The MIP-CDs/SiO2 column exhibited excellent chromatographic performance towards nucleosides, sulfonamides, benzoic acids, and some other antibiotics.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) are regarded as tailor-made receptors with specific recognition cavities for template molecules or their structural analogs [1,2]. Recently, MIPs have gained popularity in many fields, including sample preparation [3,4], chromatographic separation [5,6], sensors [7,8], catalysts [9,10], drug delivery [11,12], etc. Because of the excellent selectivity and high separation efficiency of MIPs, they have been used as stationary phases [13]. However, traditional MIPs cannot overtake commercial stationary phases yet due to notable limitations. Monolithic MIPs, as the most common type of MIPs, are synthesized by bulk polymerization. After polymerization, monolithic MIPs should be crushed and sieved to obtain suitable particles for packing in the chromatographic column. However, this process leads to the destruction of the imprinted binding cavities and irregular shapes of the materials [14]. To overcome the deficiencies of MIPs, such as heterogeneous morphology and low imprinting efficiency, reversible addition/fragmentation chain transfer (RAFT) precipitation polymerization [15], dispersion polymerization [16], and suspension polymerization [17] have been developed for the MIPs preparation. Nevertheless, MIPs synthesized via these methods mostly suffer from the slow mass transfer, irregular binding cavities, and severe template residue [18].

Surface molecularly imprinted polymers (SMIPs) possess the advantages of high specific surface area, ease of template molecules removal, quick mass transfer, and controllable binding capacity (mainly depending on the physical properties of the substrate) [19]. In addition, the tedious MIPs manipulation (mainly grinding and sieving) is avoided in the preparation of SMIPs. Recently, different functional materials (e.g. Fe3O4 nanoparticles, carbon nanomaterials, and silica microbeads) are utilized as substrates for synthesis of SMIPs [20–22]; among them, silica-based substrates, due to the ease of surface modification, excellent stability, cost-effectiveness, uniform particle size, and suitable pore structure, are widely used as chromatographic stationary phases [13]. Tuncel et al. synthesized MIPs coated silica microspheres (MIPs@SiO2) by using β-nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (β-NAD) as the template, the MIPs@SiO2 microspheres were packed into the fused silica capillary (300 μm id × 50 mm length), and the imprinting factor of the column for β-NAD was 2.07 [23]. The group of Li prepared OSMIP coated silica microbeads (OSMIP@silica gel) by using oseltamivir (OS) as the template, the imprinting factor of the resulting OSMIP@silica gel column for OS was 1.64 [24]. Lately, our group fabricated MIPs/SiO2 stationary phase via surface molecular imprinting technique; the prepared MIPs/SiO2 column showed good performance for separation of highly polar compounds (e.g. nucleosides and ginsenosides) [25]. The results suggested that the surface imprinting technique is beneficial for preparing novel stationary phases with outstanding properties.

Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional materials with a range size of 1 nm–10 nm and contain sp2-sp3 hybridized carbon atoms [26]. CDs have attracted great attention in several fields, including sensor [27], potential photocatalytic applications [28], sample pretreatment [29], and preparation of chromatographic stationary phases [30], because of optical properties [31], high specific surface area [32], nontoxicity [33] and abundant functional groups [34], etc. Recently, CDs have been gradually applied for the preparation of chromatographic stationary phases, owing to their quantum size, good stability, boosting the non-covalent interactions, such as hydrogen-bond, coordination, hydrophilic/hydrophobic, and π-π interactions [35]. The CDs based stationary phases showed good performance for separation polar compounds (Table 1 [36–40]). Qiu et al. synthesized octadecylamine and glucose-coderived hydrophobic CDs-modified silica stationary phase (Sil-Glc-OCDs), the resulting Sil-Glc-OCDs column performed well in separation of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, sulfonamides, and phenols, the results indicated that the novel stationary phase possess good shape selectivity, hydrophobicity, and week hydrogen-bonding interaction [41]. To improve the selectivity and specificity of the chromatographic stationary phases, MIPs, CDs and silica microbeads have combined to benefit from the advantages of both [21].

The good physicochemical properties of CDs (abundant functional groups: oxhydryl, formyl group and carboxyl, particle sizes: 2-5 nm), MIP (unique selectivity toward target compounds), and silica microbeads [42] (such as rich Si–OH functional groups and spherical morphology) were combined to synthesize MIP doped CDs coated silica (MIP-CDs/SiO2) stationary phase; and the procedure was illustrated schematically in Fig. 1 . The obtained MIP-CDs/SiO2 stationary phase was aimed at separating nucleosides, sulfonamides, benzoic acids, and some other antibiotics. As far as I know, it was the first time to prepare MIP-CDs/SiO2 stationary phase, and investigate their chromatographic properties in depth.

All the silica-based microbeads, including bare silica (particle size: 5 μm; pore diameter: 30 nm), and C18–SiO2 (particle size: 5 μm; pore diameter: 10 nm) were purchased from Fuji Silysia Chemical Ltd (Kasugai, Japan). Ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA, 98%) was supplied from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). Methylbenzene, 2,2-azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN), 1,4-dioxane, ethanol (EtOH), methacrylic acid (MAA), diphenyl ketone, tetrachloromethane, and 3-(trimethoxysilyl)propyl methacrylate (γ-MPS) were obtained from Tianjin Kermel Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). Cytidine (99%), adenine (98%), and cephalexin (98%) were supplied from Shanghai yuanye Bio-technology Co., Ltd (China). Stavudine (≥98%), oxytetracycline (95%), sulfanilamide (≥99%), and sulfamethoxazole (98%) were gained from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Amoxicillin crystalline (≥99%), norfloxacin (98%), sulfacetamide (98%), and sulfamethoxypyridazine (98%) were provided from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (China). Nicotinic acid (99.94%), 4-Hydroxybenzoic acid (99.98%), and benzoic acid (>99%) were supplied from Bide Pharmatech. Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Thymidine (>98%) and zidovudine (98%) were obtained from TCI Shanghai (China) and Energy Chemical (China), respectively. CDs (carbon content: 70-80 at%, particle sizes: 2-5 nm) with oxhydryl, formyl group and carboxyl were from Jiangsu XFNANO Materials Tech. Co., Ltd (Jiangsu, China). Acetic acid (HAC), acetonitrile (ACN), and methanol (MeOH) are all on the chromatography grade and were gained from Tedia company (Fairfield, OH, USA). Deionized water was supplied from Wahaha Group Co. Ltd (Hangzhou, China). AIBN was recrystallized by EtOH before using.

UV1900 (Shimadzu, Japan) was applied to detect the ultraviolet absorption intensity of the solution. Particle size distribution data of the materials was obtained via lazer particle analyzer (Marlvern MS2000, England). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images and carbon content of the materials were recorded on the SEM apparatus (S-4800, HITACHI, Japan) with a high resolution and elemental analyzer (Vario Micro, Germany), respectively. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was utilized to measure TEM images of the materials. The pore structure and Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface area of the microbeads were measured by using NOVA 4000 nitrogen adsorption instrument under −196 °C; before measurement, the materials were treated at 60 °C under vacuum for 6 h. A self-made packing machine was used to prepare chromatographic columns. The stationary phases were evaluated by the Agilent 1260 high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) system (USA), which is composed of a degasser, a 1260 auto-injector (G7129A), a 1260 quaternary pump (G7111B), a 1260 column oven (G7116A), and a 1260 DAD detector (G7115A).

Based on our previous work, the silica microbeads were activated by nitric acid solution (15%, wt.%) [43], and then, the propenyl-modified silica microbeads were prepared [25]. The detailed procedure was presented in the Electronic Supplementary Material.

Based on our previous work with some modification [25], MIP-CDs/SiO2 material was synthesized via the steps of pre-polymerization and surface imprinting polymerization process. In the pre-polymerization process, zidovudine (0.2 g) was utilized as template molecule owing to its appropriate molecular structure and good solubility in porogen, 40 μL of functional monomer (MAA) were completely dissolved in 100 mL of porogen (MeOH), and the mixture was kept in a refrigerator at 4 °C overnight. In this step, Afterward, propenyl-modified silica materials (1 g), cross-linker (400 μL EGDMA), initiator (15 mg AIBN), and 2 mg of commercial CDs were added into the pre-polymerization solution and thoroughly mixed. The mixture was kept in the water bath at 50 °C for about 12 h; after that, the temperature was raised to 60 °C and kept for 24 h. Finally, the material was washed by MeOH-HAC (9:1, v/v) six times (0.5 h each time) and ethanol thrice, until no template was detected via UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The MIP-CDs/SiO2 stationary phase was kept in a clean glass vial after drying.
Nonimprinted doped CDs coated on the silica (NIP-CDs/SiO2) stationary phase was prepared as a control according to the above protocol, without adding template molecule.

0.8 g of the stationary phase was added to 6 mL of homogenate solvent (a mixture of tetrachloromethane, methylbenzene, and 1,4-dioxane (10:1:4, V/V) and dispersed by ultrasonic wave. Then, the slurry was filled into the chromatographic columns (150 mm × 2.1 mm i.d.) at 500 bar. Within the packing process, EtOH was used as the propulsion solvent. Finally, the resulted columns were eluted by passive eluent (MeOH) for about 2 h.

The separation performance of the MIP-CDs/SiO2 column was tested in detail. In this regard, nucleosides (including 50 ppm cytidine, 20 ppm adenine, 40 ppm thymidine, 50 ppm stavudine, and 125 ppm zidovudine), sulfonamides (including 100 ppm sulfanilamide, 100 ppm sulfacetamide, 100 ppm sulfapyridine, 100 ppm sulfamethoxypyridazine, and 100 ppm sulfamethoxazole), benzoic acids (including 50 ppm nicotinic acid, 50 ppm 4-hydroxybenzoic acid, and 50 ppm benzoic acid), and a mixture of 100 ppm amoxicillin crystalline, 100 ppm cephalexin hydrate, 100 ppm oxytetracycline hydrochloride, and 100 ppm norfloxacin were selected as the probes. Furthermore, the MIP-CDs/SiO2 column was compared with other columns, such as MIP/SiO2, NIP-CDs/SiO2, and C18–SiO2. The chromatographic parameters (including retention mechanism, stability, run-to-run repeatability, and batch-to-batch repeatability) of MIP-CDs/SiO2 column were investigated, respectively. All columns were evaluated under isocratic at a flow rate of 0.2 mL/min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of nucleosides in plasma and urine
ce:section.ce:para: The standard curve of nucleosides was accomplished by adding thymidine, stavudine, and zidovudine into water. The solution of plasma with thymidine (50 ppm) and zidovudine (20 ppm), and the solution of urine with stavudine (50 ppm) were made to calculate their relative recoveries.

As shown in Fig. S1, the detection wavelength from 200 to 800 was used, and ultraviolet absorption intensity of the solution obviously decreased with the increase of washing times. After washing by the MeOH-HAC solution 3 times, the ultraviolet absorption intensity of the solutions remained stable, which demonstrating that all the templates in the materials were eluted out. In this experiment, in order to further reduce the templates and other residual compounds in the materials, the materials were washed more than 3 times [44]. In addition, the SEM images (Fig. 2 ) showed that the MIP-CDs/SiO2 microspheres were monodisperse and distributed near 5 μm. Furthermore, the TEM images presented in Fig. 2 (C–F) demonstrated that the materials coated with MIP and NIP were successfully synthesized, and CDs were successfully dopped in the polymer layer [45].

Meanwhile, the particle size distribution of MIP-CDs/SiO2 and NIP-CDs/SiO2 microspheres was from 3 μm to 6 μm Fig. 3 (A), which is in accordance with the results of SEM. The results of SEM and laser particle analyzer revealed that the particle size, sphericity, and dispersion of the MIP-CDs/SiO2 microspheres were satisfactory. Additionally, the pore structures and specific surface areas of the materials were investigated. As depicted in Fig. 3(B), the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of all the stationary phases were type-IV, demonstrating all the materials have mesoporous structure [46]. As we all know, the micro-, meso-, and macro-porous characters means that the pore size of the materials ranged from 0∼20 Ȧ, 20∼500 Ȧ, and >500 Ȧ, separately. Mesoporous materials refer to the materials possess pore size ranged from 2 nm to 50 nm [47]. As shown in Fig. S2, calculated based on the percentage of peak area, about 60% of mesoporous characters exist in the MIP-CDs/SiO2, the NIP-CDs/SiO2, and about 50% of mesoporous characters exist in the bare silica materials. These results revealed that the pore structure of the material can be influenced by the imprinting process, and this indicated that the polymerization reaction was successfully initiated in the mesopores [48]. Although the pore sizes of MIP-CDs/SiO2 microbeads significantly decreased (Table S1), they were still large enough for chromatographic separation (>7 nm) [49]. On the other hand, compared with bare silica (0%, wt.%), the significant increase of the carbon content in MIP-CDs/SiO2 (5.06%, wt.%) and NIP-CDs/SiO2 (8.27%, wt.%) (Table S1) further confirmed the successful polymerization on the surface of SiO2 beads.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chromatographic evaluation of the MIP-CDs/SiO2 stationary phase
ce:section.ce:para: Generally speaking, template molecules should possess appropriate functional groups (including hydroxy, carboxy, or amide, etc.) and good chemical stability. The functional groups can easily form complexes with the monomers and the polyreaction can be facilitated [5]. Zidovudine was selected as template molecule, owing to their appropriate chemical structure (as shown in Fig. S3), various functional groups (including functional group, azide group, carbonyl, etc.), and its good solubility and stability in pore-forming agent (acetonitrile, methyl alcohol, and ethyl alcohol, etc.).

To compare the chromatographic performance of MIP-CDs/SiO2, NIP-CDs/SiO2, MIP/SiO2, and C18–SiO2 stationary phases, nucleosides with high polarity [50] were selected as the probes. Additionally, the sulfonamides (derivatives of p-aminobenzene acid with varying amide substitution) [51], benzic acids, which are extensively used in food and drug manufacturing [52], and four other antibiotics (e.g. amoxicillin crystalline, cephalexin hydrate, oxytetracycline hydrochloride, and norfloxacin) were selected to evaluate the chromatographic properties of MIP-CDs/SiO2 column.

In recent years, MIPs have been utilized as chromatographic stationary phases by researchers [5]. It was universally known that MIPs exhibit specific recognition towards targets, in addition, MIPs have good selectivity towards other probes due to the interactions including hydrogen bond, ion-exchange and hydrophobic effect, etc [25]. In this investigation, MIP-CDs/SiO2 composite material consists of MIP, CDs, and silica-based substrate. Accordingly, the exact retention mechanism is difficult to understand fully. In this study, the mechanism including shape selectivity by the imprinted cavities, and hydrophilic interactions contribute dominantly to the retention mechanism of analytes onto the MIP-CDs/SiO2 stationary phase. Additionally, other kinds of interactions like hydrogen-bond, hydrophilic, and π-π could participate in the retention mechanism. As displayed in Fig. 4 (A) , the relative retention factor (k’) of the nucleosides decreased gradually with the increasing MeOH content in the mobile phase. This indicates that a typical reversed-phase liquid chromatography (RPLC) mode was developed. According to the results of Fig. 4 (B), the concentration of HAC in the mobile phase has a significant impact on the k’ of the probes; the eluted order of the analytes can be even changed by the HAC concentration. This further confirmed the abnormal and complex retention mechanism of the MIP-CDs/SiO2 column for the nucleosides.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Separation efficiency of different columns for nucleosides
ce:section.ce:para: The separation efficiency of MIP-CDs/SiO2 column with chromatographic columns packed with MIP/SiO2, NIP-CDs/SiO2, and commercial C18–SiO2 was investigated. To this aim, cytidine, adenine, thymidine, stavudine, and zidovudine were used as target analytes to evaluate the chromatographic properties of all the columns. In all experiments, the mobile phase consisted of MeOH/aqueous solution with 0.2% HAC (0.3/99.7, v/v) and the flow rate was set at 0.2 mL/min.

As illustrated in Fig. 5 and Table S2, the chromatographic performances of the columns packed with various stationary phases (MIP-CDs/SiO2, MIP/SiO2, and NIP-CDs/SiO2) were evaluated for separation of nucleosides. As shown in Table S2, the chromatographic parameters (including resolution (Rs) and column efficiency (N, plates/m)) of the MIP-CDs/SiO2 column were better than those of MIP/SiO2 column and NIP-CDs/SiO2 column. The results revealed that the chromatographic performances of the columns can be greatly improved by combination of MIPs and CDs, the proper reason is that the CDs possess outstanding properties including small size, high surface to volume ratio, facile functionalization, unique separation selectivity, moderate adsorption performance, good chemical stability, and water solubility [41]; in addition, the MIPs have unique selectivity. Additionally, for the NIP-CDs/SiO2 column, the Rs of nucleosides (between thymidine and stavudine) was 1.49, and the tailing factor of 5.91 for zidovudine was observed. The results revealed that the combination of MIPs, CDs, and silica substrates is favorable for improving the chromatographic properties of the stationary phase. Meanwhile, the separation performance of the commercial C18–SiO2 column for nucleosides was inferior. The possible reason is that the polarity of nucleosides is too high and the C18–SiO2 column interacts with analytes mainly via hydrophobic interactions. Moreover, commercial Diol-SiO2 stationary phase and commercial Amino-SiO2 stationary phase were packed into column, and they were applied for separation of nucleosides. Nucleosides can't be separated on these commercial polar stationary phases packed columns. All these results confirmed that the MIP-CDs/SiO2 column has promise for use in the chromatographic separation of nucleosides.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chromatographic performance of MIP-CDs/SiO2 column for antibiotics, and benzoic acids
ce:section.ce:para: Five sulfonamides, including sulfanilamide, sulfacetamide, sulfapyridine, sulfamethoxypyridazine, and sulfamethoxazole were used to evaluate the chromatographic separation ability of the MIP-CDs/SiO2 column. In Fig. 6 (A) and Table S3, these sulfonamides were wholly separated by MIP-CDs/SiO2 column when the mobile phase of ACN/aqueous solution with 0.3% HAC (5/95, v/v) was used. On the MIP-CDs/SiO2 column, the N of sulfonamides was no less than 10,350 N/m, and the Rs value of them were no less than 2.15. As seen in Fig. 6, the elution order of the sulfonamides on the MIP-CDs/SiO2 column was consistent with that of the commercial C18–SiO2 column, and self-made materials (MIP-CDs/SiO2, MIP/SiO2, and NIP-CDs/SiO2) packed columns. The retention time of the analytes on the C18–SiO2 was much longer than other kinds of analytes, suggests that the columns are hydrophobic, and the hydrophobicity of C18–SiO2 column was much stronger than other columns. Besides, compared with Fig. 7 (B-F), MIP-CDs/SiO2 column worked well in the separation of other kinds of antibiotics (amoxicillin crystalline, cephalexin hydrate, oxytetracycline hydrochloride, and norfloxacin) (Fig. 7 and Table S4). better separation performance was obtained on the MIP-CDs/SiO2 column.

In addition, compared with other kinds of columns (including C18–SiO2, MIP/SiO2, NIP-CDs/SiO2, Diol-SiO2, and Amino-SiO2 columns), the MIP-CDs/SiO2 column showed much better separation performance, indicating the MIP-CDs/SiO2 column exhibited a high potential to separate benzoic acids (Fig. S4 and Table S5). These excellent results can be attributed to the unique selectivity of MIP, good physicochemical properties of CDs, and satisfactory characteristics of silica microbeads substrates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and reproducibility of the MIP-CDs/SiO2 column
ce:section.ce:para: The stability and reproducibility of the MIP-CDs/SiO2 column are important for its practical applications. In this regard, five nucleosides were used to evaluate the stability and reproducibility of the MIP-CDs/SiO2 column. Four main parameters, including N, peak area, k’, and tailing factor were tracked to visualize column degradation over ten days. According to the results of Fig. 8 and Fig. S5, the peak area, tailing factor, and k’ of nucleosides on the MIP-CDs/SiO2 column decreased by about 25%, and the N of the analytes decreased by less than 15% (the error bars have been added) within eight days; as shown in Fig. 8, ten days later, the N and peak area decreased dramatically, while k’ and tailing factor kept essentially constant; these results mean that the MIP-CDs/SiO2 column degradation decreased slowly after eight days evaluation. After MIP-CDs/SiO2 column stability evaluation, the carbon content of the resulting MIP-CDs/SiO2 material was 4.314% (wt.%), lower than the carbon content of the MIP-CDs/SiO2 material before chromatographic evaluation. This result implied that the main reasons for decreasing efficiency of this column are leaching of CDs or polymer structure damage in the chromatographic evaluation process.

Furthermore, the reproducibility and repeatability of the column were investigated systemically. As can be seen in Fig. S6, the relative standard deviation (RSD) values were less than 4.72% and 1.69% for the retention time of three different batches of columns and one column, respectively, indicating satisfactory reproducibility and repeatability of MIP-CDs/SiO2 column. According to the aforementioned results, it can conclude that MIP-CDs/SiO2 column possesses excellent separation properties. Compared with other kinds of carbon nanoparticles (Table 1) or MIPs based stationary phases [25], the MIP-CDs/SiO2 stationary phase is more suitable for separation of nucleosides, sulfonamides, benzoic acids, and other types of antibiotics.

In this study, although chromatographic properties of the MIP-CDs/SiO2 stationary phase have been greatly improved by combination of MIPs, CDs and silica microbeads, however, its N, resolution, selectivity, peak symmetry are still not ideal. In the future, novel functional monomers, templates, and novel strategies should be developed to improve the chromatographic performances of MIP-CDs/SiO2 stationary phase.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of nucleosides in plasma and urine
ce:section.ce:para: The standard curve and regression equation of nucleosides on the MIP-CDs/SiO2 column were presented in Fig. S7. The relative recoveries of thymidine and zidovudine in plasma ranged from 98.3% to 98.9% (RSD = 0.28%) and 96.2%–96.6% (RSD = 0.20%), respectively. In addition, relative recoveries of stavudine in urine was 89.42%–91.22% (RSD = 0.87%). The results demonstrated that the MIP-CDs/SiO2 column possess great potential in real sample analysis.

In conclusion, this work reports the synthesis and application of MIP-CDs/SiO2 stationary phase. The synthesis strategy is favorable for large-scale production. The MIP-CDs/SiO2 stationary phase exhibited regular spherical morphology, high specific surface area, acceptable pore structure, and good stability. Furthermore, the MIP-CDs/SiO2 column showed some potential for chromatographic separating nucleosides, sulfonamides, benzoic acids, and other types of antibiotics. Additionally, the resulting MIP-CDs/SiO2 column benefits from the advantages of acceptable stability, repeatability, and reproducibility in chromatographic separation. However, the chromatographic performance of the MIP-CDs/SiO2 stationary phases should be further improved via using new functional monomers, templates, and good synthesis approaches.

Peijun Chai: carried out the experiments. Xuhui Geng: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Ruirui Zhu: carried out the experiments. Wenpu Wu: Investigation, &, Editing. Xuesong Wang: Data curation. Jinhua Li: Writing – review & editing. Longwen Fu: Investigation. Hongdan Wang: Writing – original draft. Wanhui Liu: Writing – review & editing. Lingxin Chen: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Zhihua Song: Writing – original draft, preparation, Review, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The investigation is part of the project named the Science and Technology Innovation Development Plan of Yantai City of China (2020MSGY112), and The Youth Innovation Promotion Association of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (No. 2019186). Additionally, it was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province of China (ZR2020QC011), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22176210), Yantai University Student Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program, and the National Demonstration Center for Experimental Pharmacy Education (Yantai University).

ce:section.ce:para:
